content.body:
## Why Transform Coding?

**Raw pixels problem:**
* Neighboring pixels are **correlated** (similar) → redundant info → hard to compress

**Transform solution:**
* Convert to frequency domain → values become **uncorrelated**
* Energy concentrates in **few coefficients**
* Small coefficients → throw away → compression!

---

## Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)

* **Low frequency:** values change slowly (smooth regions)
* **High frequency:** values change quickly (edges, details)

DCT decomposes signal into **cosine waves** of different frequencies:
* **F(0)** = DC component (average — constant part)
* **F(u > 0)** = AC components (variations)

### 1D DCT Formula

$$F(u) = \frac{C(u)}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{7} \cos\left(\frac{(2i+1)u\pi}{16}\right) f(i)$$

$$C(u) = \begin{cases} 1/\sqrt{2} & u=0 \\ 1 & u>0 \end{cases}$$

---

## DCT Examples

**Example 1 — Constant [100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100]:**
* F(0) ≈ 283 (DC, **all** energy here)
* F(1) to F(7) = 0
* Constant signal → all energy in one coefficient!

**Example 2 — Alternating [20, −20, 20, −20, 20, −20, 20, −20]:**
* F(0) = 0 (average is zero)
* F(4) is large — rapid alternation → energy in high freq

**Example 3 — Ramp [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]:**
* F(0) ≈ 9.90 (DC average)
* F(1) ≈ −6.44 (main slope)
* F(2) to F(7) ≈ 0 (small)
* Most info in just 2 coefficients!

---

## Key insight

For natural signals:
* **Few low-freq coefficients = BIG** → keep
* **Many high-freq coefficients = SMALL** → throw away

→ This is how compression happens

flashcards.q: Why doesn't compressing raw pixels work well? What does transform coding fix?
flashcards.a: Raw pixels are correlated — neighbors are similar, so each value is redundant. Transform coding converts them into uncorrelated frequency components where most energy concentrates in a few large values. The rest can be zeroed without visible damage.

flashcards.q: What is spatial frequency? Low vs high?
flashcards.a: How many times pixel values change across a block. Low = values change slowly (smooth areas: sky, walls). High = values change rapidly (edges, textures, fine details).

flashcards.q: What is the DC component? What is an AC component?
flashcards.a: DC = F(0) = average value of all pixels in the block. Usually the largest coefficient. AC = all other coefficients F(u>0) = the variations. Higher u → higher frequency → finer detail.

flashcards.q: What happens when you apply DCT to a constant block [100,100,...,100]?
flashcards.a: F(0) is large (~283), all other coefficients are zero. No variation → no AC components. All energy in the DC (average) term.

flashcards.q: What happens when you apply DCT to a ramp [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]?
flashcards.a: F(0)≈9.90 (DC average), F(1)≈−6.44 (main slope), the rest are tiny. Most info in just 2 coefficients. Zero the remaining 6 → still a good ramp approximation. This is the core idea of DCT compression.

flashcards.q: In a 2D DCT of an 8×8 block, what is at the top-left? Bottom-right?
flashcards.a: Top-left F(0,0) = DC component = average brightness (usually largest). Bottom-right F(7,7) = highest frequency in both directions = finest checkerboard pattern (usually near zero in natural images).

flashcards.q: If you zero some DCT coefficients before doing IDCT, what happens?
flashcards.a: You get an approximation of the original block, not a perfect copy — that's the lossy part. The more coefficients you zero, the more compression and more quality loss. Zeroing small high-frequency coefficients causes minimal visible damage.

flashcards.q: What does the IDCT do step by step?
flashcards.a: Builds the signal back by adding components one at a time: DC → rough constant. Add AC1 → main shape. Add AC2, AC3... → more detail. Add all → perfect reconstruction. First few components carry most info.

problems.q: A DCT is applied to [100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100, 100]. Describe the output without calculating. Why?
problems.a:
F(0) will be large (≈283) and **all other coefficients = 0**.

A constant signal has no variation — zero frequency content beyond DC. DCT perfectly captures this: all energy in one value. This block compresses to a single number.
problems.hint: Constant = zero variation = only DC component.

problems.q: Why do natural images compress well with DCT? What happens to high-frequency coefficients?
problems.a:
Natural images have **smooth regions** — neighboring pixels change slowly. When DCT is applied to an 8×8 block from a smooth region, most energy concentrates in the **low-frequency coefficients** (top-left of the 8×8 coefficient grid).

High-frequency coefficients are near zero → can be set to zero (quantized away) with little visible quality loss.
problems.hint: Smooth = low frequency = energy concentrated at top-left. High-freq coefficients ≈ 0 → discard.

problems.q: For the ramp [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7], DCT gives F(0)≈9.90, F(1)≈−6.44, rest≈0. If you only keep F(0) and F(1) and set the rest to zero, what happens when you apply IDCT?
problems.a:
You get an **approximation** of the ramp — the IDCT reconstructs using only the DC (average) and the main slope component.

The reconstruction will look like a smooth ramp, very close to the original [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7], because those two coefficients carry almost all the information. The small high-freq coefficients contribute only minor refinements.
problems.hint: IDCT with zeros = approximate reconstruction. Less coefficients = more lossy but still recognizable.
